imageAlt: Montage of the 1982 Falklands War showing British troops, warships, and Harrier aircraft during the South Atlantic campaign
imageCaption: Falklands War montage, 1982. Clockwise from top: British troops advance on Stanley; HMS Hermes task force; a Sea Harrier; Argentine prisoners at Goose Green. Wikimedia Commons / Public Domain.

longForm.title: Seventy-Four Days: The Falklands War, April–June 1982
longForm.excerpt: On 2 April 1982, Argentine forces seized the Falkland Islands. Britain dispatched a naval task force 8,000 miles. Seventy-four days later, 904 men were dead.

paragraphs.body: At 4:30 in the morning on 2 April 1982, Argentine commandos came ashore at Mullet Creek on East Falkland. They moved on the Royal Marine barracks at Moody Brook first — found it empty, the garrison already redeployed to defend Government House. Governor Rex Hunt had ordered Major Mike Norman's sixty-eight Royal Marines to resist. They did. The Argentines took casualties they hadn't expected. Then the numbers became irrelevant. By mid-morning the island had fallen, Hunt had been expelled, and Argentina's military junta had achieved its central objective: the Malvinas, as Buenos Aires called them, were Argentine.

In London, the news reached the War Cabinet on a Friday afternoon. Admiral Sir Henry Leach, the First Sea Lord, was called from the House of Commons. He told Margaret Thatcher that yes, a naval task force could be assembled, and yes, it could recapture the islands. The decision was made over a weekend. Within forty-eight hours, the carrier group centred on HMS Hermes and HMS Invincible was being loaded and provisioned. The task force that eventually sailed numbered over 100 ships and 28,000 personnel — assembled and dispatched with a speed that surprised even its own commanders. It had 8,000 miles to travel and no guarantee of what would be waiting when it arrived.

The first military action came on 25 April. Operation PARAQUET retook South Georgia, the smaller island 800 miles southeast of the Falklands. SAS and SBS teams landed in atrocious weather — two helicopters were lost to a white-out on the Fortuna Glacier, the crews rescued under fire. The Argentine submarine Santa Fe was caught on the surface, disabled by helicopter-launched missiles and depth charges, and ran aground. South Georgia was retaken the same day. One British soldier was wounded. The task force sailed on.

May 2. HMS Conqueror, a nuclear-powered submarine, had been tracking the Argentine cruiser ARA General Belgrano for two days. Belgrano was sailing outside the 200-mile exclusion zone Britain had declared around the islands, but the Rules of Engagement were extended that afternoon on the authority of the War Cabinet. Conqueror fired two Mk8 torpedoes — a weapon dating from the Second World War. Both struck. Belgrano sank in thirty-six minutes. Of her 1,093 crew, 323 were killed. The sinking became the most contested act of the war. Argentina argued the cruiser posed no immediate threat; Britain maintained she was part of a two-pronged attack formation that had to be neutralised. Argentinian naval sorties ceased after 2 May. The navy did not venture out again.

Two days later, on 4 May, an Argentine Super Étendard aircraft fired an AM39 Exocet missile at HMS Sheffield. Sheffield had no warning. The missile struck at sea-skimming speed, failed to detonate, but started a fire that could not be controlled. Twenty of her crew were killed. Sheffield was the first Royal Navy warship lost in combat since 1945. The Exocet threat — fast, radar-guided, fired from well beyond visual range — became the defining tactical problem of the campaign. Britain had no reliable defence against it. The answer was to sink the aircraft carrying them before they could fire, or to get inside the missile's minimum engagement range. Neither was straightforward.

The main landings came on 21 May. Operation SUTTON put 3,000 men ashore at San Carlos Water, a protected bay on the west coast of East Falkland. Argentine air power responded immediately. For three days, wave after wave of A-4 Skyhawks and Daggers flew low and fast through the hills, pressing through intense anti-aircraft fire. HMS Ardent, Antelope, and Coventry were sunk. The container ship Atlantic Conveyor was struck by Exocet on 25 May, destroying three of the four heavy-lift Chinook helicopters the ground campaign depended on. The landings succeeded. The British were ashore, in numbers, and the air threat was absorbing Argentine losses faster than Argentine sortie rates could sustain it. Of thirty-two aircraft lost to British defences, most were on the way out, not the way in.

The ground campaign opened at Goose Green, 28–29 May. The 2nd Battalion Parachute Regiment (2 PARA) attacked Argentine positions held by elements of the 12th Infantry Regiment and air force personnel — approximately 1,000 defenders against 600 British troops. Lieutenant Colonel Herbert Jones led from the front during the assault on Boca House, was killed charging a trench line singlehandedly, and was awarded a posthumous Victoria Cross. His adjutant Major Keeble took command. The battle lasted seventeen hours. When it ended, Air Vice Commodore Wilson Pedroza surrendered with 1,200 men — double the size of the force that had just defeated him.

The final advance on Stanley was a series of night attacks on the ring of mountains defending the town. On the night of 11–12 June: Mount Longdon fell to 3 PARA, Two Sisters to 45 Commando, Mount Harriet to 42 Commando. Two nights later: Mount Tumbledown fell to the 2nd Scots Guards after close-quarter fighting that lasted until dawn, and Mount William to the 1/7th Gurkha Rifles. On Longdon, Sergeant Ian McKay of 3 PARA was killed charging a machine-gun position. Posthumous Victoria Cross — the second of the campaign. By the morning of 14 June, Argentine positions inside Stanley were untenable.

General Mario Menéndez signed the instrument of surrender in Stanley at 9:00 p.m. on 14 June 1982. The conflict had lasted seventy-four days. British dead: 255. Argentine dead: 649. Three Falkland Island civilians were killed, two by Argentine fire, one by a British shell. The Royal Navy lost six ships sunk and twelve damaged. Argentina lost an estimated 100 aircraft. The Belgrano accounted for more than one-third of Argentine total fatalities.

The campaign was an operational improvisation from beginning to end. The task force sailed without complete intelligence on Argentine positions, without adequate air cover, and with a logistics chain stretched to its limit. The ground forces won by night assault — movement and violence of action compensating for numerical disadvantage. The Argentine air force fought with genuine professionalism and came closer to breaking the task force than the official British narrative acknowledged for years. What stopped them was not British air defence but Argentine bomb fuzes, set too shallow to detonate on a fast, low-level attack run. Of the Exocets that struck British ships, several failed to explode. Had they functioned as designed, the campaign's outcome might have been different.

shortForm.title: Come On, 2 PARA: The Battle of Goose Green, May 28–29, 1982
shortForm.excerpt: Six hundred British paratroopers attacked a thousand Argentine defenders at Goose Green. Their commanding officer was killed in the assault. They won anyway.

paragraphs.body: The briefing said 150 Argentines at Goose Green. On the night of 28 May 1982, 2nd Battalion, Parachute Regiment — 600 men — moved off Sussex Mountain in darkness toward Argentine positions across three miles of open ground. The real number was closer to 1,000. The intelligence was wrong. Nobody turned back.

Lieutenant Colonel Herbert Jones — 'H' to everyone — was killed at approximately 10:00 a.m. on 29 May, during an assault on Argentine positions near Boca House. His own Company had stalled under fire. Jones ran forward alone, charged a trench, and was shot from behind by a flanking position he had not seen. He was forty-two years old. His death did not break the battalion. Major Chris Keeble took command. The attack continued. By late afternoon the Argentine position was collapsing.

Air Vice Commodore Wilson Pedroza surrendered at approximately 4:00 p.m. on 29 May with 1,200 men under his command. Seventeen British had been killed, thirty-five wounded. Forty-seven Argentines were dead. The disparity in prisoner numbers — twice the attacking force — reflected the scale of the intelligence failure and the extent of the victory. Jones was awarded the Victoria Cross posthumously. The citation noted he had 'inspired his battalion to overcome a position of great strength.'

Three weeks later, Sergeant Ian McKay of 3 PARA was killed in the same manner at Mount Longdon — charging a machine-gun position that had pinned down his platoon. Posthumous Victoria Cross. Two VCs in a single campaign, both to paratroopers, both awarded for the same act: running at the thing that was killing everyone else. The Falklands was the first conflict since Korea to produce British VCs in ground combat.

onThisDay.headline: HMS Conqueror Sinks ARA General Belgrano, Killing 323 Argentine Sailors
onThisDay.body: On 2 May 1982, the British nuclear-powered submarine HMS Conqueror torpedoed the Argentine cruiser ARA General Belgrano south of the Falkland Islands. The ship sank within thirty-six minutes. Of her 1,093 crew, 323 were killed — more than half of all Argentine deaths in the Falklands War. The sinking remains the only confirmed combat sinking of a vessel by a nuclear-powered submarine in history.
